title: Docker Basics: Concepts, Configuration, and First Workloads
excerpt: Learn how Docker works under the hood, how containers are built and run, and how to create your first production-ready workloads using Dockerfiles.

body.value:
Docker containers are lightweight, portable environments that allow applications to run consistently across different systems. Unlike traditional virtual machines, containers share the host operating system kernel, making them faster and more efficient.

Because containers are defined using text-based configuration (like Dockerfiles), they can be versioned, automated, and reproduced with high reliability.

value.title: Key Idea
value.text: Containers are not full virtual machines — they are isolated processes sharing the same OS kernel.

body.value: With a single Docker command, you can launch a fully working web server. Docker will automatically download the required image, create a container, and expose it to the outside world.

value.caption: Running an Apache container
value.code:
docker run -d \
  --name app0 \
  -e TZ=UTC \
  -p 8080:80 \
  httpd:latest

body.value:
This command:

  • Runs a container in detached mode
  • Maps port 8080 (host) to 80 (container)
  • Uses a prebuilt Apache image

Once started, the web server is immediately accessible via http://localhost:8080.

value.caption: Docker Run Flow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Run command] --> B[Check local image]
 B -->|Not found| C[Pull from Docker Hub]
 C --> D[Create container]
 D --> E[Start process]
 E --> F[Expose ports]

body.value: Docker is more than just a CLI tool — it is a complete platform consisting of multiple components working together.

value.caption: Docker Architecture
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 CLI[Docker CLI] --> API[REST API]
 API --> Daemon[Docker Daemon]
 Daemon --> Runtime[Container Runtime]
 Runtime --> Containers[Running Containers]
 Daemon --> Images[Docker Images]
 Images --> Registry[Docker Registry]

body.value:
Key components:

  • Docker Engine – the core platform
  • Docker Daemon – manages containers
  • CLI – user interface
  • Images – blueprints for containers
  • Containers – running instances
  • Registry – stores images (e.g. Docker Hub)

value.title: Important
value.text: Docker relies on the Linux kernel. On Windows/macOS, it runs inside a lightweight Linux VM.

body.value: A Docker image is a read-only template. A container is a running instance of that image.

value.caption: Image to Container Lifecycle
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 Dockerfile --> Image
 Image --> Container
 Container -->|run| Process

value.caption: Example Dockerfile
value.code:
FROM ubuntu:24.04

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y apache2

COPY index.html /var/www/html/index.html

EXPOSE 80

CMD ["apache2ctl", "-D", "FOREGROUND"]

body.value:
This Dockerfile:

  • Uses Ubuntu as base image
  • Installs Apache
  • Copies custom HTML
  • Exposes port 80
  • Starts the web server

value.caption: Build and run the image
value.code:
docker buildx build -t my-server .
docker run -d -p 8080:80 my-server

body.value: The Docker daemon is the core service that manages containers. Its behavior is configured using the daemon.json file.

value.caption: Example daemon.json

body.value: Changes require restarting Docker:

value.caption: Restart Docker
value.code: sudo systemctl restart docker

value.caption: Useful commands
value.code:
docker ps
docker ps -a
docker stop <container>
docker logs <container>
docker info

body.value: As applications grow, multiple containers must work together. This is where orchestration tools come in.

value.caption: Container Orchestration Options
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Single Container] --> B[Docker Compose]
 B --> C[Docker Swarm]
 C --> D[Kubernetes]

body.value:
• Docker Compose – multi-container apps on one host
  • Docker Swarm – clustering across hosts
  • Kubernetes – enterprise-grade orchestration

value.title: Final Thought
value.text: If you can define your infrastructure as code, you can automate everything.

title: Containerizing a Python Web App with Docker: From Local Flask App to Reusable Image
excerpt: Learn how to take a Python Flask application running locally, package it into a Docker image, run it as a container, troubleshoot it with logs and stats, and prepare it for sharing through image registries.

value.title: Start by Understanding How the App Runs Without Docker

body.value:
The best way to containerize an application is to first understand how it runs outside Docker. If you already know the exact steps required to start the app locally, writing a Dockerfile becomes much easier because the Dockerfile is simply a repeatable description of those same steps.

In this example, the application is a small Python Flask web app. Locally, the workflow is straightforward: create a virtual environment, install dependencies, and run the application entry point.

This idea is bigger than Python. The same principle applies to .NET, Node.js, Java, Go, and other runtimes. Before you containerize, make sure you understand the local startup path.

value.title: Core Principle
value.text: A Dockerfile should mirror the real steps required to run the app manually. If you cannot explain the local startup process, containerization will feel confusing.

value.caption: Typical local workflow for a Flask app
value.code:
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
python app/main.py

value.caption: From source code to running local application
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Clone repository] --> B[Create virtual environment]
 B --> C[Activate environment]
 C --> D[Install dependencies]
 D --> E[Run main.py]
 E --> F[Open Flask app in browser]

value.title: Turn Manual Runtime Steps into a Dockerfile

body.value:
Once the local process is clear, the next step is to translate it into a Docker image build. In Docker, you do not describe a running machine. You describe how to build an image that contains everything needed to run the application.

The first important decision is the base image. For a Python app, that usually means starting from an official Python image. The course uses a slim variant, which keeps the image smaller while still including Python and pip.

Instead of cloning the repository inside the image, Docker simply copies files from your existing working directory into the image build context.

value.caption: Dockerfile for a Flask application using pip
value.code:
FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

EXPOSE 3000

CMD ["python", "app/main.py"]

body.value:
This Dockerfile mirrors the local setup:

  • FROM gives you Python and pip
  • WORKDIR defines the working folder inside the image
  • COPY requirements.txt brings in dependency metadata first
  • RUN pip install installs the dependencies during image build
  • COPY . . adds the rest of the application source code
  • CMD defines the process that starts when the container runs

The virtual environment step disappears because the container itself already provides process isolation. In most simple containerized Python apps, a separate venv inside the container is unnecessary.

value.title: Why copy requirements first?
value.text: Separating dependency installation from source code copy improves Docker layer reuse. If only the app code changes, Docker can often reuse the already-built dependency layer.

value.caption: How the Dockerfile maps to the original local workflow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 Local[Local steps] --> L1[Create environment]
 Local --> L2[Install dependencies]
 Local --> L3[Run app]
 Docker[Docker build/runtime] --> D1[Base image already includes Python]
 Docker --> D2[RUN pip install -r requirements.txt]
 Docker --> D3[CMD python app/main.py]
 L1 -.replaced by container isolation.-> D1
 L2 --> D2
 L3 --> D3

value.title: Build the Image and Run the Container

body.value: After writing the Dockerfile, the next step is to build an image and run a container from it. The build process executes each Dockerfile instruction and creates a layered image. That image then becomes the blueprint for one or more containers.

value.caption: Build and run the Flask image
value.code:
docker image build -t flask-web .

docker container run --rm -p 3001:3000 flask-web

body.value:
Here, port 3001 is the host port and 3000 is the container port. The Flask process listens on port 3000 inside the container, but users connect to port 3001 on the host machine.

The --rm flag tells Docker to remove the container automatically after it stops. That is useful for quick test runs because it keeps the local environment clean.

value.caption: Image and container runtime flow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Dockerfile] --> B[docker image build]
 B --> C[Docker image: flask-web]
 C --> D[docker container run]
 D --> E[Running container]
 E --> F[Port mapping 3001:3000]
 F --> G[Browser request to localhost:3001]

value.title: Foreground Containers, Detached Containers, and Removal Behavior

body.value:
In the course, containers are first run in the foreground. That means the terminal remains attached to the main process, so you can see logs directly and stop the application with Ctrl+C.

Later, the container is run in detached mode. Detached mode is closer to how services are commonly run because the container continues in the background and the terminal is freed for other tasks.

value.caption: Run a named container in detached mode
value.code: docker container run -d --name web1 -p 3001:3000 flask-web

value.caption: Monitor and stop the container
value.code:
docker container ps
docker container stop web1
docker container start web1

body.value:
A key distinction appears here:

  • With --rm, the container is deleted when it stops
  • Without --rm, the stopped container remains and can be restarted later

This is useful during debugging because keeping stopped containers lets you inspect their state, logs, and configuration.

value.title: Practical Rule
value.text: Use throwaway containers for quick tests, and named detached containers when you want to inspect, restart, or manage a service over time.

body.value:
The course also demonstrates the same Flask app using the uv package manager instead of pip. The containerization pattern stays the same, but the build inputs and commands change.

Instead of requirements.txt, the project uses pyproject.toml and a lock file. The base image must also provide the uv executable.

value.caption: Example pattern for a uv-based Dockerfile
value.code:
FROM ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:python3.12-bookworm-slim

WORKDIR /src

COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./
RUN uv sync

COPY . .

EXPOSE 3000

CMD ["uv", "run", "app/main.py"]

body.value:
The important lesson is not the specific package manager. It is the repeatable pattern:

  • choose a base image that includes the correct runtime tooling
  • copy dependency manifests first
  • install dependencies during build
  • copy source code
  • define a startup command

Once an image works locally, it can be shared through a registry such as Docker Hub or GitHub Container Registry. A registry hosts repositories, and each repository contains one or more tagged images.

The course shows how the official Python base image comes from Docker Hub, while the uv image comes from GitHub Container Registry. The same mechanics apply whether you are consuming images or publishing your own.

value.caption: Registry hierarchy
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Registry] --> B[Repository]
 B --> C[Image tags]
 C --> D[latest]
 C --> E[v2]
 C --> F[v3.0.1]

value.caption: Tag and push an image to Docker Hub
value.code:
docker image tag flask-web weshigbee/flask-web:latest
docker login
docker image push weshigbee/flask-web:latest

body.value:
Tagging does not duplicate the image contents. It simply adds another name that points to the same image ID. That is why the same image can appear under multiple names locally.

When pushing, Docker uploads image layers. If most layers are unchanged, Docker reuses what already exists in the registry and only pushes what is new.

value.title: Why tags matter
value.text: Tags are how you distinguish application versions. They let you deploy, test, and roll back with intention instead of relying on an ambiguous latest image.

value.title: Use Tags to Represent Real Application Versions

body.value:
When the application changes, build a new image with a new tag. In the course, the Flask app header is updated to show a new version, and the new image is built with a version-specific tag like v2 or v3.0.1.

This makes application evolution visible and controlled. Instead of overwriting one image mentally, you preserve clear deployable versions.

value.caption: Build versioned images
value.code:
docker image build -t weshigbee/flask-web:v2 .
docker image build -t weshigbee/flask-web:v3.0.1 .

body.value: When a container is already running an older version, you do not "upgrade" that container in place. Instead, you remove the old container and create a new one from the new image.

value.caption: Replace a running container with a new image version
value.code:
docker container rm -f web1
docker container run -d --name web1 -p 3001:3000 weshigbee/flask-web:v3

value.title: Use Container Logs to Debug Runtime Failures

body.value:
The course adds a small /adder/<a>/<b> endpoint to demonstrate debugging. The endpoint fails because Flask expects a valid response type, but the code returns a float directly instead of a string or response object.

When the page returns an internal server error, the first place to look is the container logs.

value.caption: Inspect logs for a running container
value.code: docker container logs web1

value.caption: Buggy Flask route and fix

body.value: The important lesson is that container logs are not just infrastructure output. They are often your fastest path to the real application error.

value.title: Development Workflow Tip
value.text: For small code fixes, rebuilding images repeatedly is slow. During development, run the app locally with Python. Use containers when you are packaging and validating a deployable build.

body.value:
Applications often behave differently in development and production. Flask is a classic example because debug mode changes error output and enables helpful development behavior such as reloading and detailed stack traces.

The course shows how to pass FLASK_DEBUG=true into the container at runtime.

value.caption: Set an environment variable when starting a container
value.code: docker container run --rm -e FLASK_DEBUG=true -p 3001:3000 weshigbee/flask-web:v3

body.value: You can also define a default environment variable in the Dockerfile using ENV. That bakes a default configuration into the image, though runtime values can still override it.

value.caption: Define a default environment variable in the image
value.code: ENV FLASK_DEBUG=true

value.caption: How environment defaults and overrides work
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Dockerfile ENV FLASK_DEBUG=true] --> B[Image config default]
 B --> C[Container starts]
 D[Runtime flag -e FLASK_DEBUG=false] --> C
 C --> E[Runtime value wins over image default]

value.title: Inspect Image Metadata to Understand What an Image Really Contains

body.value:
Docker images contain not just files, but configuration metadata. The course uses docker image inspect to show details such as environment variables, exposed ports, and the startup command.

This is useful when you want to verify that a build produced the image you expected.

value.caption: Inspect image metadata
value.code: docker image inspect weshigbee/flask-web:v3.1

body.value:
Two categories of layers are especially important conceptually:

  • filesystem contributions such as copied files or installed packages
  • configuration contributions such as CMD, ENV, or EXPOSE

Understanding that split helps explain why images are efficient, reusable, and composable.

value.title: Use Stats to Observe CPU and Memory Usage

body.value:
The course closes with runtime monitoring and simple load testing. Docker provides a built-in way to inspect live container resource usage using docker container stats.

Combined with a simple load tool such as Apache Bench, this gives fast feedback about whether your application remains lightweight under higher request volume.

value.caption: Watch container resource usage
value.code: docker container stats

value.caption: Simple load testing with Apache Bench
value.code: ab -n 10000 -c 4 http://localhost:3001/

body.value: Watching CPU and memory usage during load helps you reason about capacity. Even in a simple example, it demonstrates an important production mindset: containerization is not only about packaging — it is also about observability and repeatable operations.

This course is not just about writing a Dockerfile. It teaches a practical sequence for containerizing a real application:

  • understand how the app runs locally
  • translate those steps into a Dockerfile
  • build an image and run a container
  • manage the container lifecycle
  • debug with logs
  • configure behavior with environment variables
  • tag and share images through registries
  • observe runtime health with stats

Once these skills are comfortable, you are prepared for the next layer of container work: multi-container development, orchestration, CI/CD integration, and cloud deployment.

value.title: Final Takeaway
value.text: A container image is a repeatable delivery artifact. The real value of Docker appears when you treat your app’s runtime, dependencies, configuration, and deployment process as something explicit and reproducible.

title: Managing Container Images: Dockerfiles, Registries, Security, and Kubernetes
excerpt: Learn how container images work, how to build them with Dockerfiles, how to host a private Docker Registry, how to secure image distribution, and how Kubernetes pulls images from private repositories.

body.value:
Container images are the foundation of modern Docker workflows. They package an operating system layer, application code, dependencies, and startup instructions into a predictable, portable unit that can be launched anywhere Docker or a compatible runtime is available.

The real value of images is not only portability, but repeatability. Instead of documenting how to install software by hand, you define the environment once in code and rebuild it whenever needed. That makes development, testing, and production much more consistent.

value.title: Core Principle
value.text: An image should be immutable. When it runs as a container, Docker adds a separate writable runtime layer on top of the read-only image layers.

value.caption: Image and container relationship
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Dockerfile] --> B[Docker Image]
    B --> C[Read-only layers]
    B --> D[Container start]
    D --> E[Writable container layer]
    E --> F[Runtime changes and logs]

body.value:
A Docker image is made of layers. A base layer might contain Ubuntu or Alpine Linux. Additional layers can install packages, add files, configure services, or define startup commands. Because layers are reusable, Docker can share them across many images and containers on the same host.

This layered model improves both speed and storage efficiency. If multiple images reuse the same base OS or dependency layer, Docker does not need to download or store identical data over and over again.

That same design also supports predictability. If an image is built correctly, it provides the same environment on a laptop, a server, or in production.

value.caption: Useful image inspection commands
value.code:
docker images
docker search alpine
docker pull alpine
docker history alpine

body.value:
docker images shows locally available images. docker search helps discover public images on Docker Hub. docker pull downloads an image, and docker history reveals the layers and commands used to build it.

When pulling from public repositories, prefer official or well-maintained images. Public images of unknown quality may contain outdated software, poor configuration, or even malicious content.

Images are usually built from Dockerfiles. A Dockerfile is a declarative text file where each instruction describes the desired final state of the image. Common instructions include FROM, ENV, RUN, COPY, EXPOSE, and CMD.

A typical example is building an Ubuntu-based Apache image, installing the web server, copying a custom index.html, exposing port 80, and defining the foreground process that should keep the container alive.

value.caption: Example Dockerfile for a simple Apache web server
value.code:
FROM ubuntu:24.04

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y apache2

COPY index.html /var/www/html/index.html

EXPOSE 80

CMD ["apache2ctl", "-D", "FOREGROUND"]

value.caption: Build and run the image
value.code:
docker build -t webserver .
docker run -d --name webserver01 -p 8080:80 webserver
docker ps
curl http://localhost:8080

body.value:
Beyond the basics, Dockerfiles support many other instructions. LABEL adds metadata, USER changes the user context, WORKDIR sets the working directory, ENTRYPOINT defines a non-overridable startup command, HEALTHCHECK verifies runtime health, and ARG provides build-time values.

One useful distinction is COPY versus ADD. Both move files into the image, but ADD can also fetch remote URLs and unpack archives automatically. In most simple cases, COPY is clearer and more predictable.

value.title: Reliability, Version Pinning, and Layer Optimization

body.value:
Reliable images are built from Dockerfiles, not captured from running containers with docker commit. Committing a live container may preserve unknown state, temporary files, manual edits, or log artifacts. That makes the result much harder to reproduce or trust.

Version pinning is also important. Using ubuntu:latest or debian:latest feels convenient, but it can unexpectedly change the software stack when upstream maintainers move the tag forward. A safer approach is to pin a known image tag like debian:12 or ubuntu:24.04, then deliberately rebuild when you are ready for updates.

At the same time, pinning versions means you must monitor upstream security updates yourself. Predictability and patch freshness must be balanced intentionally.

value.title: Practical Rule
value.text: Do not rely on latest for important workloads. Pin exact image versions, then rebuild on your own schedule after validation.

value.caption: Why fewer RUN instructions often produce smaller images
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Separate RUN commands] --> B[More layers]
    B --> C[Larger image]
    D[Combined RUN command] --> E[Fewer layers]
    E --> F[Smaller image]

body.value:
Layer count also affects image size. Every RUN instruction creates a new layer. Combining related shell commands into a single RUN statement often reduces the final size because intermediate files do not persist across as many layers.

That is why image optimization often includes combining package installation, download, extraction, and cleanup steps into one layer.

value.caption: Layer-friendly package installation pattern
value.code:
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y curl && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

body.value:
Once an image works locally, the next step is sharing it. Docker Hub is the default public image registry and is often good enough for demos, open-source projects, and team collaboration. After authenticating with docker login, you can retag a local image to include your Docker Hub namespace and push it to the remote repository.

That lets other users pull the image with a single command, assuming the repository is public or they have access to the private repo.

value.caption: Tag and push an image to Docker Hub
value.code:
docker login
docker tag webserver your-dockerhub-user/webserver:latest
docker push your-dockerhub-user/webserver:latest

body.value: Cloud platforms offer alternatives such as Amazon ECR, Azure Container Registry, and Google Container Registry. The exact service changes, but the workflow is similar: build, tag, authenticate, and push.

For private networks, internal development, or stricter control over distribution, you can run your own Docker Registry. One simple way is to run the official registry image as a container on a host that already has Docker installed.

On the server side, port 5000 is commonly exposed for the registry service. On Docker clients, if the registry is not encrypted yet, Docker must be configured to trust that registry as insecure before pushing or pulling images.

value.caption: Start a basic Docker Registry
value.code:
docker run -d \
  --name registry \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  registry:2

value.caption: Client-side insecure registry configuration
Tag, push, and pull from a private registry
value.code:
docker tag webserver 192.168.1.50:5000/webserver:latest
docker push 192.168.1.50:5000/webserver:latest
docker pull 192.168.1.50:5000/webserver:latest

value.caption: Private registry workflow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Developer workstation] --> B[Build image]
    B --> C[Tag for private registry]
    C --> D[Push to registry]
    D --> E[Registry server]
    E --> F[Other client or cluster]
    F --> G[Pull image]

value.title: Avoid Losing Images Stored in the Registry

body.value:
A registry container without persistent storage is fragile. If the container is removed, its image catalog disappears with it. The fix is to mount a host directory into the registry container so repository data survives restarts and replacement.

It is also a good idea to add a restart policy so the registry comes back automatically after a host reboot.

value.caption: Registry with persistent host storage
value.code:
mkdir -p /var/lib/registry-data

docker run -d \
  --name registry \
  --restart always \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  -v /var/lib/registry-data:/var/lib/registry \
  registry:2

value.title: Encrypting and Authenticating a Private Registry

body.value:
A production-ready registry should not rely on plain HTTP. Traffic between Docker clients and the registry should be encrypted with TLS, typically using certificates issued by a trusted certificate authority such as Let's Encrypt.

Once a domain name points to the registry host, the certificate files can be mounted into the registry container and exposed through environment configuration so the registry serves HTTPS traffic securely.

Encryption protects image transfers in transit, but it does not control who is allowed to use the registry. For that, basic authentication can be added with an htpasswd file containing usernames and hashed passwords.

value.caption: Generate an htpasswd file for registry authentication
value.code:
mkdir -p ~/auth

docker run --rm \
  --entrypoint htpasswd \
  registry:2 -Bbn myuser mypassword > ~/auth/htpasswd

value.caption: Run Docker Registry with TLS and authentication
value.code:
docker run -d \
  --name registry \
  --restart always \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  -v /var/lib/registry-data:/var/lib/registry \
  -v /opt/registry/certs:/certs \
  -v /home/ubuntu/auth:/auth \
  -e REGISTRY_HTTP_TLS_CERTIFICATE=/certs/domain.crt \
  -e REGISTRY_HTTP_TLS_KEY=/certs/domain.key \
  -e REGISTRY_AUTH=htpasswd \
  -e REGISTRY_AUTH_HTPASSWD_REALM='Registry Realm' \
  -e REGISTRY_AUTH_HTPASSWD_PATH=/auth/htpasswd \
  registry:2

value.caption: Authenticate from a Docker client
value.code:
docker login registry.example.com:5000
docker push registry.example.com:5000/webserver:latest

value.title: Security Reminder
value.text: TLS protects the connection. Authentication protects access. You usually need both for a real private registry.

value.title: Verifying Images with Docker Content Trust

body.value:
Transport encryption proves that traffic is protected during transfer, but it does not guarantee that an image is signed and trusted. Docker Content Trust adds signing and verification to image workflows so clients can validate that an image came from a trusted publisher and has not been tampered with.

On the client side, Content Trust is enabled through an environment variable. Once enabled, unsigned images can fail to pull, and signed pushes require access to the correct key material and passphrase.

value.caption: Enable Docker Content Trust
value.code:
export DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST=1
echo $DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST

value.caption: Image trust flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Publisher
    participant Registry
    participant Client
    Publisher->>Registry: Push signed image
    Registry-->>Client: Serve image and trust metadata
    Client->>Client: Verify signature
    Client->>Registry: Pull only if trusted

body.value: This trust model is especially useful when images are shared across teams or environments. It reduces the risk of silently consuming modified or malicious content.

value.title: Pulling Images from Private Registries in Kubernetes

body.value:
Kubernetes consumes container images the same way Docker does, but pod creation is usually defined in YAML rather than directly through CLI flags. Public images are simple to run, but private images require Kubernetes to authenticate to the remote registry.

The standard way to do that is to create a registry secret and reference it through imagePullSecrets in the pod or deployment specification.

value.caption: Create a Docker registry secret in Kubernetes
value.code:
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com:5000 \
  --docker-username=myuser \
  --docker-password=mypassword

value.caption: Pod spec that pulls from a private registry
value.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: private-webserver
spec:
  containers:
    - name: webserver
      image: registry.example.com:5000/webserver:latest
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
  imagePullSecrets:
    - name: regcred

value.caption: Apply and inspect the pod
value.code:
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
kubectl get pods
kubectl describe pod private-webserver

body.value: One practical caution is credentials handling. Commands entered in a shell can be recorded in history files such as .bash_history. That means secret creation commands may leave traces on disk. In secure environments, use stricter operational hygiene, dedicated secret management workflows, or more controlled shell practices.

value.title: Kubernetes Admission Controllers and Image Policies

body.value:
Kubernetes can enforce image-related rules through admission controllers. These are API-level controls that inspect or modify requests before workloads are accepted by the cluster.

Two image-related controllers are especially relevant. AlwaysPullImages forces pods to pull images from their original source instead of reusing cached copies, which helps in multi-tenant environments. ImagePolicyWebhook allows external policy systems to decide whether a requested image should be allowed.

value.caption: Admission control idea
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Pod create request] --> B[Kubernetes API Server]
    B --> C[Admission Controller]
    C -->|Allowed| D[Pull image]
    C -->|Denied| E[Reject workload]

value.title: Big Picture
value.text: Image security is not only about building images. It also includes registry transport security, authentication, trust validation, and cluster-side enforcement.

body.value:
Managing container images well means understanding the full lifecycle: how images are built, how their layers affect size and speed, how they are distributed through public or private registries, and how security is enforced at each stage.

A strong workflow usually includes reproducible Dockerfiles, pinned base images, optimized layers, a properly configured registry with persistent storage, TLS encryption, registry authentication, trust validation, and secure Kubernetes pull configuration.

Once these pieces are in place, images become more than packaging artifacts. They become a reliable delivery mechanism for infrastructure and application behavior.

title: From Docker Compose to Swarm and GKE: Deploying a Multi-Container Microservices App Step by Step
excerpt: Learn how to take a small microservices application from local Docker Compose development to Docker Swarm orchestration and finally to Kubernetes on Google Kubernetes Engine, including databases, networking, scaling, ingress, and monitoring.

body.value:
Modern applications are often split into separate services instead of being delivered as one large monolith. A typical example is a web front end, an API, and a database, each with its own responsibility. This makes the system easier to evolve, easier to scale, and easier to deploy in repeatable environments.

Containers fit this model extremely well because each service can be packaged independently with its own runtime, dependencies, and configuration. In practice, that means you can run the same application locally with Docker Compose, move it to a Docker Swarm cluster for orchestration, and later deploy the same container images to Kubernetes.

value.title: Core Idea
value.text: The application stays conceptually the same across environments: a frontend talks to an API, the API talks to a database, and orchestration tools manage networking, scaling, and recovery.

value.caption: Multi-Container Application Structure
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 Browser[Browser] --> Web[Web Frontend / NGINX]
 Web --> API[Catalog API]
 API --> DB[(Postgres Database)]

value.title: Starting with docker init and docker compose up

body.value:
The first step is containerizing the API. For a .NET application, docker init can inspect the project, detect the platform, generate a Dockerfile, and suggest a Compose-based startup configuration. This is a fast way to bootstrap container support without writing every file by hand.

After generating the Docker-related files, docker compose up builds the image and starts a container from it. Docker pulls any required base images, runs the Dockerfile instructions, creates the custom application image, and then launches the container.

value.caption: Bootstrap and run the API locally
value.code:
docker init
docker compose up

body.value: In the course example, the API is exposed on port 9000. Once the container is running, the application becomes reachable through http://localhost:9000. At this stage, however, the API still fails on database-driven endpoints because only the API container exists.

value.caption: What docker compose up Does
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[docker compose up] --> B[Read compose file]
 B --> C[Build custom image]
 C --> D[Pull base images if needed]
 D --> E[Create container]
 E --> F[Attach network]
 F --> G[Start application]

body.value:
A 500 error from the API usually means the application has started, but one of its dependencies is missing. In this case, the logs reveal that the API cannot connect to a database. The solution is to define a second service in the Compose file for Postgres and wire the API to it using environment variables.

One of the big advantages of Compose is built-in service discovery. If the database service is named db, the API can connect to that hostname directly because Compose places both services on the same network automatically.

value.caption: Compose file with API and Postgres
value.code:
services:
  api:
    build:
      context: ./catalog-api/src
    ports:
      - "9000:8080"
    environment:
      ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection: Host=db;Port=5432;Database=library_catalog;Username=postgres;Password=postgres

  db:
    image: postgres:17.2
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: library_catalog
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./database/init/init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql:ro

volumes:
  postgres_data:

body.value:
This configuration introduces two critical concepts. First, the named volume preserves database files outside the container lifecycle, which protects data when containers are recreated. Second, the mounted SQL script initializes the schema and seed data automatically the first time the database starts.

The API container does not need to know an IP address. It only needs the service name db, because Compose manages internal DNS resolution between containers on the project network.

value.caption: Docker Compose Networking and Storage
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 API[API Container] -->|Host=db| DB[Postgres Container]
 DB --> VOL[(Named Volume: postgres_data)]
 DB --> INIT[init.sql mounted at startup]
 API --> NET[Compose Network]
 DB --> NET

value.title: Why the Service Name Matters
value.text: Inside a Compose application, containers communicate through service names, not localhost. From the API container, localhost means the API container itself, not the database.

value.title: Why docker compose down Is Important

body.value:
Once the Compose file changes significantly, especially after renaming services, it is often safer to tear the environment down and recreate it cleanly. Otherwise, old containers, networks, or images may remain behind as orphaned resources.

docker compose down removes the running containers and the project network. With additional flags, it can also remove images. This makes it easy to reset the local environment and verify that the configuration still works from scratch.

value.caption: Clean up and rebuild
value.code:
docker compose down --rmi local
docker compose up

body.value: After recreation, the API and database containers should start together. If the initialization script runs successfully and the connection string is correct, database-backed API endpoints begin working immediately.

The final piece of the local application is the frontend. In the course example, the web application is served by NGINX using static files and a custom configuration. This introduces a third service to the stack: web.

Unlike the Postgres service, the web service is built from a local directory because it has its own Dockerfile and custom files. The container is published on host port 8080, while the API remains on 9000.

value.caption: Add a web service
value.code:
services:
  web:
    build:
      context: ./web
    ports:
      - "8080:80"

body.value: At this point the local system consists of three containers: web, API, and database. The browser talks to the web container, the web container proxies or calls the API, and the API reads from Postgres. This is a classic microservices-style composition, even though it is still running on a single machine.

value.caption: Three-Service Local Stack
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 User[User Browser] -->|localhost:8080| Web[NGINX Web]
 User -->|localhost:9000| API[API]
 Web --> API
 API --> DB[(Postgres)]

body.value:
One of the most practical benefits of Compose is repeatability. You can destroy the containers with docker compose down, confirm the application is no longer reachable, and bring everything back with docker compose up. Because the Postgres data lives in a named volume, the database contents survive this cycle.

This illustrates an essential principle in containerized development: containers are disposable, but state must be handled deliberately through volumes or external services.

value.title: Key Lesson
value.text: Containers should be easy to destroy and recreate. Durable data should live outside the container filesystem.

value.title: Moving from One Host to a Docker Swarm Cluster

body.value:
Local Compose works well for development, but production-like environments usually require multiple machines. Docker Swarm provides clustering and orchestration while staying close to the Docker CLI model. A Swarm is initialized on one manager node, and worker nodes join using a generated token.

Once the cluster is created, services can be scheduled across nodes. A service is the Swarm abstraction for one or more containers managed as a unit.

value.caption: Initialize a Swarm and inspect nodes
value.code:
docker swarm init
docker node ls

body.value: In the course demo, one Raspberry Pi acts as the manager and two more act as workers. After joining them, the cluster becomes capable of running distributed services across all three machines.

value.caption: Docker Swarm Cluster
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 M[Manager Node] --- W1[Worker Node 1]
 M --- W2[Worker Node 2]
 M --> S[Swarm Control Plane]
 S --> T1[Service Tasks]
 S --> T2[Replicas across nodes]

value.title: Creating, Visualizing, and Scaling Swarm Services

body.value:
The next step is deploying services into the cluster. A visualization service can help show where tasks are placed, and then a second service can demonstrate scaling and load balancing. In Swarm terminology, each running instance of a service is a task, effectively a managed container replica.

When a service is scaled from one replica to two or more replicas, Swarm automatically distributes them across nodes when possible. Requests sent to the published port are load balanced across the available tasks.

value.caption: Create and scale a Swarm service
value.code:
docker service create --name echo -p 8081:8080 hashicorp/http-echo

docker service scale echo=2
docker service ps echo

value.caption: Swarm Load Balancing Across Replicas
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 Client[Client Request] --> LB[Swarm Routing Mesh]
 LB --> R1[echo.1]
 LB --> R2[echo.2]
 R1 --> N1[Node pi3]
 R2 --> N2[Node pi4]

body.value:
This is one of Swarm's strongest operational features: scaling is declarative. You specify the desired replica count, and Swarm creates or removes tasks until the actual state matches the requested state.

The same mechanism also gives you self-healing. If a container dies, Swarm notices that the desired replica count is no longer satisfied and starts a replacement task automatically.

value.title: Self-Healing in Practice
value.text: If you manually remove a running container that belongs to a Swarm service, Swarm immediately schedules a new one so the service returns to its desired state.

value.title: Turning a Compose File into a Swarm Stack

body.value:
Managing several separate docker service create commands quickly becomes cumbersome for a real multi-container application. A better approach is to adapt the Compose configuration into a Swarm stack file. This keeps the architecture declarative and lets Docker deploy the whole application as a named stack.

The main differences are operational. Custom images need proper names so they can be pushed to a registry like Docker Hub, configuration files can be injected as Swarm configs, and stateful services such as databases may need placement constraints to ensure they always land on the intended node.

value.caption: Build, push, and deploy a stack
value.code:
docker compose -f stack.yaml build
docker compose -f stack.yaml push

docker stack deploy -c stack.yaml staging

docker stack services staging
docker stack ps staging

body.value: After deployment, the services are prefixed with the stack name, such as staging_api, staging_db, and staging_web. This makes it possible to host multiple environments on the same cluster, for example staging and production, without service-name collisions.

value.caption: Stack Deployment Flow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[stack.yaml] --> B[Build custom images]
 B --> C[Push images to registry]
 C --> D[docker stack deploy]
 D --> E[Create services]
 E --> F[Create overlay network]
 E --> G[Create configs]
 E --> H[Create volumes]

value.title: Why Tasks Can Fail During Startup

body.value:
Distributed orchestration introduces a timing issue that does not always appear locally: one service may start before another dependency is ready. In the course example, the NGINX web service can fail during startup because it tries to reach the API before the API is available. Swarm treats that as a failed task and retries it.

That is why stack-level task listings can show failed historical tasks even when the application is now healthy. Looking at service logs helps explain whether the issue was a transient dependency problem or a real configuration error.

value.caption: Investigate failed tasks
value.code:
docker stack ps staging
docker service ps staging_web
docker service logs staging_web

body.value: A message such as host not found in upstream api from NGINX usually means the frontend started before the API service was ready to resolve or accept traffic. In many orchestrated systems, initial restart attempts are normal while the full dependency graph comes online.

value.title: Networks, Volumes, and Configs in Swarm

body.value:
Swarm manages more than just containers. It also creates cluster-aware networks for service communication, volumes for persistent data, and configs for externalized files such as bootstrap SQL scripts. These are first-class resources and can be inspected independently.

This is especially important for stateful workloads. In the course example, the database volume exists only on the node that hosts the Postgres task, while the SQL init script is stored as a Swarm config that can be injected into the container when needed.

value.caption: Inspect Swarm resources
value.code:
docker network ls
docker volume ls
docker config ls
docker config inspect staging_init_sql

value.title: Deploying the Same Architecture to GKE

body.value:
After proving the application locally and in Swarm, the next step is deploying it to Kubernetes on Google Kubernetes Engine. The main idea remains the same, but the resources change. Instead of a Compose file or stack file, Kubernetes uses manifests to define deployments, services, pods, and ingress rules.

In the course example, an Autopilot GKE cluster is created, credentials are fetched with the Google Cloud CLI, and a dedicated namespace is prepared for the application. This isolates the workload and keeps related resources grouped together.

value.caption: Create a cluster and prepare a namespace
value.code:
gcloud container clusters create-auto prod

gcloud container clusters get-credentials prod
kubectl create namespace library1
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=library1

value.caption: Kubernetes Version of the Application
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 Internet[External Traffic] --> Ingress[Kubernetes Ingress]
 Ingress --> WebSvc[web Service]
 Ingress --> ApiSvc[api Service /api]
 WebSvc --> WebPod[Web Pod]
 ApiSvc --> ApiPod[API Pod]
 ApiPod --> DbSvc[postgres Service]
 DbSvc --> DbPod[(Postgres Pod)]

body.value:
As in local Compose, the database is created first. The Kubernetes manifest defines the Postgres container image, environment variables, and a Service resource that exposes the pod internally. A temporary port-forward allows local tools such as psql to connect for testing and initialization.

Once connected, the SQL bootstrap script can be applied to create the books table and its initial data. This mirrors the earlier Compose example, but here it is done through Kubernetes resources and CLI workflows.

value.caption: Apply Postgres manifest and connect locally
value.code:
kubectl apply -f postgres.yaml
kubectl get all
kubectl port-forward svc/postgres 5432:5432
psql -U postgres -d library_catalog -f ./init.sql

value.title: Practical Detail
value.text: In the demo, recreating the pod removes the database contents because a persistent volume is not configured. In production, you would attach persistent storage so data survives pod replacement.

value.title: Exposing the API and Web App Through an Ingress

body.value:
After the database is ready, the API and web manifests are applied. Each one defines a container image and an internal Kubernetes Service. An Ingress resource then provides external routing. In this setup, requests to /api are sent to the API service, while other paths are sent to the web service.

This is an important architectural concept because it centralizes external access while keeping the underlying services internal to the cluster.

value.caption: Deploy API, web, and ingress
value.code:
kubectl apply -f api.yaml
kubectl apply -f web.yaml
kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml
kubectl get all
kubectl get ingresses

body.value: Initially the Ingress may not have an external address. That is normal while the cloud load balancer is being provisioned. Once the external IP appears, opening it in the browser should show the library catalog, and hitting the /api/books path should return the API response.

value.title: Adding Postgres Exporter for Prometheus Metrics

body.value:
One of the strengths of containerized systems is how easy it is to bolt on supporting capabilities. The course demonstrates this by adding a second container to the Postgres pod: a Prometheus-compatible exporter. This sidecar exposes metrics on port 9187 without changing the database image itself.

Once the pod is recreated, a port-forward can expose that metrics endpoint locally. From there, Prometheus-style metrics can be inspected directly in the browser or with curl.

value.caption: Check exporter metrics
value.code:
kubectl apply -f postgres.yaml
kubectl get pods
kubectl port-forward pod/postgres 9187:9187
curl http://localhost:9187/metrics

value.caption: Postgres Pod with Exporter Sidecar
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 Pod[Postgres Pod] --> DB[Postgres Container]
 Pod --> EXP[postgres-exporter Container]
 EXP --> METRICS[/metrics endpoint]
 Prometheus[Prometheus or curl] --> METRICS

body.value:
A particularly useful metric in the demo is database size. By capturing the metrics output before and after creating the application table, you can see the database grow. That proves the exporter is correctly scraping internal Postgres data and exposing it in a machine-readable format.

In a real production system, these metrics would be scraped continuously by Prometheus and visualized in Grafana, with alerts added for thresholds such as storage growth, scrape failures, or connection issues.

value.caption: Capture metrics before and after a database change
value.code:
curl http://localhost:9187/metrics > metrics1.log
psql -U postgres -d library_catalog -f ./init.sql
curl http://localhost:9187/metrics > metrics2.log
diff metrics1.log metrics2.log

value.title: What This End-to-End Journey Really Teaches

body.value:
This workflow demonstrates a very practical deployment progression. Docker Compose is excellent for local development and fast iteration. Docker Swarm introduces clustering, scaling, and self-healing with minimal conceptual overhead. Kubernetes on GKE takes the same containerized architecture into a cloud-native production model with manifests, ingress, and stronger ecosystem support.

The most important lesson is that containers give you a portable unit of deployment, but the real operational maturity comes from how you manage dependencies, data persistence, networking, startup ordering, observability, and recovery. Those concerns exist in every environment; the tooling simply evolves as the system grows.

value.caption: Deployment Maturity Path
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 A[Single Container] --> B[Docker Compose\nLocal multi-service app]
 B --> C[Docker Swarm\nCluster orchestration]
 C --> D[Kubernetes / GKE\nCloud-native production]

value.title: Final Thought
value.text: The architecture does not fundamentally change as you move from local development to orchestration platforms. What changes is the level of automation, resilience, and operational control around the same set of services.

title: Preparing Docker Applications for Production
excerpt: A practical guide to preparing Docker applications for production by externalizing configuration, surfacing logs, modeling health and readiness checks, and routing traffic with reverse proxies in Docker and Kubernetes.

body.value: Why production-ready containers need more than a Dockerfile

Getting an application to run in a container is only the starting point. A production platform such as Docker Swarm or Kubernetes can start, stop, and replace containers, but unless your application is designed to work with the platform, the platform only sees a black box.

To get the real benefits of containers in production—portability, centralized operations, self-healing, safer deployments, and clean traffic routing—you need to make your application images and deployment model integrate with the platform.

The core ideas are straightforward:

  • read configuration from the container environment rather than baking environment-specific settings into images,
  • surface application logs as container logs,
  • report health and readiness so the platform can make good decisions,
  • and route incoming traffic through a reverse proxy or ingress layer.

value.caption: Production integration model for containers
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Docker Image] --> B[Container Runtime]
    B --> C[Configuration from platform]
    B --> D[Logs to stdout/stderr]
    B --> E[Health and readiness signals]
    B --> F[Traffic routed through proxy or ingress]
    C --> G[Portable deployment across environments]
    D --> H[Centralized logging]
    E --> I[Self-healing and zero-downtime rollout]
    F --> J[Multi-app hosting, HTTPS, caching, load balancing]

body.value: The four production concerns

Preparing Docker applications for production usually comes down to four concerns:

  1. Configuration – the same image should work in dev, test, and production with different runtime settings.
  2. Logging – logs should be collected centrally and searchable across all components.
  3. Self-healing – the platform should detect unhealthy components and take corrective action.
  4. Traffic management – incoming requests should be routed safely and efficiently to the right containers.

This post walks through all four and shows how the same ideas apply in Docker Compose and Kubernetes.
1. Configuration should come from the container environment

A production image should not be rebuilt just because the environment changes. The image should contain application binaries and sensible defaults, while the environment-specific settings come from the platform at runtime.

The two main configuration sources exposed by the container environment are:

  • environment variables,
  • files mounted into the container filesystem.

A good configuration model merges sources in a clear precedence order.

value.caption: Configuration precedence inside a container
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Default config bundled in image] --> B[Override config file mounted into container]
    B --> C[Environment variables]
    C --> D[Final runtime settings used by app]

body.value: This model is powerful because it lets you ship one immutable image and change behavior per environment without editing application code or rebuilding artifacts.

value.caption: Docker run with environment variables
value.code:
docker run \
  -e APP_ENV=prod \
  -e METRICS_ENABLED=false \
  my-app

value.caption: Docker run with a mounted override config
value.code:
docker run \
  -v $(pwd)/config:/app/config-override \
  my-app

body.value: Using built-in config libraries when the app supports layered configuration

Modern application platforms usually have libraries that already support hierarchical configuration. In the course examples:

  • the Go application used Viper,
  • the Node.js application used node-config,
  • and the Java application required extra help because it expected a single config file.

When the app platform supports it natively, the implementation is clean. The application reads defaults from a local file, then looks for an override file in another path, and finally allows environment variables to override everything else.

value.caption: Compose example with environment variables and mounted config
value.code:
services:
  app:
    image: my-app
    environment:
      APP_ENV: PROD
      METRICS_ENABLED: "false"
    volumes:
      - ./config:/app/config-override

body.value: This is exactly what you want in production: a declarative model where runtime configuration is stored outside the image and versioned alongside deployment definitions.
When the app cannot merge configuration: the Config Loader pattern

Not every application can read from multiple sources. Older systems often expect exactly one file in exactly one location. That does not mean they cannot be made production-ready in containers.

The solution is a small configuration loader utility packaged into the image. The loader reads:

  • the default config,
  • an optional override file,
  • and selected environment variables,

then writes a single final config file where the application expects it.

value.caption: Config loader pattern for legacy applications
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Default config in image] --> D[Config Loader utility]
    B[Mounted override config] --> D
    C[Environment variables] --> D
    D --> E[Generated final config file]
    E --> F[Application startup]

value.caption: Container startup that prepares config before running the app
value.code: CMD config-loader && java -jar app.jar

body.value:
This pattern is especially useful for Spring Boot apps expecting a properties file, legacy .NET Framework applications expecting XML config, or any application that does not natively support layered configuration.

The important outcome is consistency: all application components can be configured the same way from the platform, even if their internal configuration models differ.
Kubernetes ConfigMaps and Secrets

Kubernetes makes externalized configuration a first-class concept. Instead of keeping configuration only in Compose files or host folders, you store configuration as platform objects:

  • ConfigMaps for non-sensitive settings,
  • Secrets for sensitive values such as API keys or credentials.

These objects can be injected into containers either as files or as environment variables.

value.caption: How Kubernetes injects configuration into a pod
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[ConfigMap] --> B[Pod]
    A2[Secret] --> B
    B --> C[Mounted file in container filesystem]
    B --> D[Environment variable]
    C --> E[Application config]
    D --> E

value.caption: Kubernetes ConfigMap example
value.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  config.toml: |
    [environment]
    name = "PROD"
    metrics_enabled = false

value.caption: Kubernetes Secret example
value.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: app-secret
type: Opaque
stringData:
  API_KEY: DEMO_KEY

body.value: This is one of the biggest operational advantages of containers: the application image lifecycle is separated from the configuration lifecycle. The same tested image can be promoted through test and production environments, while each environment supplies its own ConfigMaps and Secrets.

value.text: Best practice: promote the same image through every environment. Change configuration with environment variables, ConfigMaps, and Secrets—not by rebuilding the image.

body.value: 2. Logs must become container logs

In production, centralized logging is one of the main reasons you want your applications integrated with the container platform. But the platform can only collect what it can see.

Docker and Kubernetes both treat stdout and stderr as the container log stream. That means if your application already writes useful logs to the console, you are in a good place. If it writes logs somewhere else, such as files, syslog, ETW, or the Windows Event Log, the platform will not see them automatically.

value.caption: Standard container logging model
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Application writes to stdout or stderr] --> B[Container runtime captures logs]
    B --> C[Log collector]
    C --> D[Central storage]
    D --> E[Search and dashboards]

body.value: Most modern frameworks support console logging out of the box. What usually matters is configuring the right logging level and making sure your application actually writes useful logs.

value.caption: Example Winston config writing logs to stdout

body.value: If your application is silent, container logging does not help. The container platform does not invent logs; it only forwards what the application emits.
Centralized logging with Fluent Bit, Elasticsearch, and Kibana

A common production logging stack in Kubernetes is the EFK stack:

  • Fluentd or Fluent Bit collects logs,
  • Elasticsearch stores them,
  • Kibana provides search and visualization.

Fluent Bit is often preferred in clusters because it is lighter than Fluentd while still handling log collection effectively.

value.caption: EFK logging pipeline
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Application and system containers] --> B[Fluent Bit on each node]
    B --> C[Elasticsearch]
    C --> D[Kibana]

body.value:
In Kubernetes, Fluent Bit usually runs as a DaemonSet, which means one collector pod runs on each node. It reads the container log files from the host filesystem, enriches them with Kubernetes metadata such as namespace, pod, and container name, and forwards them to Elasticsearch.

This lets you search across:

  • application logs,
  • Kubernetes system logs,
  • all replicas of all services,
  • and all environments in the cluster.

value.caption: Example Fluent Bit output to Elasticsearch
value.code:
[OUTPUT]
    Name  es
    Match kube.default.*
    Host  elasticsearch
    Index app-logs

body.value: When apps write logs to files: the log relay pattern

Some applications write logs to a hidden sink, typically a file. Docker does not know about that file, so from the outside the container appears to have no logs even though the application is logging internally.

If you cannot change the application to log to stdout directly, add a log relay.

value.caption: Log relay for applications writing to files
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Application writes log file] --> B[Relay utility tails file]
    B --> C[Writes log lines to stdout]
    C --> D[Container runtime captures logs]

value.caption: Simple Linux startup command using tail as relay
value.code: CMD app & tail -f /logs/app.log

body.value:
This works, but it is not perfect. The foreground process becomes the relay rather than the application, which can confuse basic liveness assumptions. If the app crashes but the relay keeps running, the container may still appear alive. If the relay fails while the app is healthy, the container may exit. The pattern is useful, but it should be treated as a compatibility strategy, not the ideal design.

For Windows containers, Microsoft provides LogMonitor for this exact purpose. It can relay logs from files, Event Log, or ETW into stdout without needing to rewrite the application.
Sidecar logging in Kubernetes

Kubernetes offers a cleaner alternative to running multiple processes in one container: the sidecar pattern.

Instead of bundling the app and relay in one container, place them in the same pod:

  • one container runs the application and writes logs to a shared volume,
  • the second container tails the shared log files and writes them to stdout.

value.caption: Sidecar logging pattern in Kubernetes
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[App container] --> B[Shared volume]
    B --> C[Logger sidecar]
    C --> D[Pod stdout logs]
    D --> E[Fluent Bit]

body.value: This keeps the application container single-purpose while still making logs visible to the platform.
3. Self-healing requires health and readiness signals

One of the most attractive promises of containers is self-healing: if something goes wrong, the platform fixes it automatically. But the platform can only act on the signals it receives.

The default signal is weak: if the container process is still running, the platform assumes the container is healthy. That is often not enough. A web app can be running but still be stuck, overloaded, misconfigured, or returning errors.

To make an application truly self-healing, you need explicit checks.

value.caption: Liveness vs readiness

body.value: Healthchecks in Docker

Docker images support a built-in HEALTHCHECK instruction. The runtime executes the healthcheck command on a schedule and interprets the exit code:

  • 0 means healthy,
  • non-zero means unhealthy.

A simple HTTP-based healthcheck can be implemented with curl.

value.caption: Docker healthcheck using curl
value.code: HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s CMD curl --fail http://localhost/health || exit 1

body.value:
This is easy, but it has a trade-off: curl must be installed in the image. That increases image size, attack surface, and patching responsibility.

A healthier long-term approach is to build the check using the same runtime your application already uses.
Custom health checks using the application runtime

Instead of installing a third-party tool just for healthchecks, create a small utility or script in the application's own runtime:

  • Node.js script for a Node.js app,
  • Java class for a Java app,
  • Go binary for a Go app,
  • .NET utility for a .NET app.

This avoids extra dependencies and lets you express richer logic.

value.caption: Node.js-based Docker healthcheck
value.code: HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s CMD ["node", "healthcheck.js"]

value.caption: Example healthcheck logic in Node.js

body.value: The check can share the same configuration model as the main application, which makes it easier to keep behavior consistent across environments.
Readiness checks and dependency checks

Readiness is not the same as liveness. Liveness asks whether the app is still healthy. Readiness asks whether it is ready to do useful work.

A common readiness concern is dependency availability. For example, if an API requires a database or a third-party service to be reachable before it can function, the container should not accept traffic until that dependency is available.

In Docker, there is no dedicated readiness concept like Kubernetes provides, so a common pattern is to run a dependency check in the startup sequence.

value.caption: Startup sequence with config loading and dependency validation
value.code: CMD config-loader && dependency-check && java -jar app.jar

body.value: If the dependency check fails, the container exits. In a platform like Swarm or Kubernetes, that exit becomes a signal to restart or replace the workload.
Kubernetes liveness probes and readiness probes

Kubernetes does not use Docker image healthchecks directly. Instead, it expects health and readiness to be modeled explicitly in pod specs using probes.

The main probe types are:

  • livenessProbe – if it fails, Kubernetes restarts the container,
  • readinessProbe – if it fails, Kubernetes stops routing traffic to that pod but does not restart it,
  • startupProbe – often used for slow-starting applications, though not central in the course examples.

value.caption: Kubernetes HTTP liveness probe
value.code:
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 80
  periodSeconds: 10

value.caption: Kubernetes exec liveness probe
value.code:
livenessProbe:
  exec:
    command:
      - node
      - healthcheck.js
  initialDelaySeconds: 10
  periodSeconds: 30
  failureThreshold: 2

value.caption: Kubernetes readiness probe
value.code:
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /ready
    port: 8080
  periodSeconds: 10

body.value: Readiness is especially important during rolling updates. New pods are started first, but they receive no traffic until they report ready. That is one of the main building blocks behind zero-downtime deployments.

value.caption: How readiness enables safe rollouts
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Old version pods serving traffic] --> B[New pods start]
    B --> C{Ready?}
    C -- No --> D[No traffic sent]
    C -- Yes --> E[Traffic gradually shifts]
    E --> F[Old pods removed]

body.value: A critical warning: bad probes can break healthy systems

Health and readiness probes are powerful, but they can be dangerous if designed poorly. A probe that calls a third-party service too frequently can create the outage it is trying to prevent.

For example, if a liveness probe repeatedly calls an external API with strict rate limits, it may trigger those limits, start failing, and cause Kubernetes to restart every pod. Instead of improving resilience, the probe takes the service offline.

Probes should be:

  • fast,
  • cheap,
  • reliable,
  • and ideally focused on local application state rather than expensive external workflows.

value.text: Probe design rule: prefer checking local application health or cached dependency state. Avoid frequent liveness probes that directly hammer fragile third-party services.

body.value: 4. Reverse proxies solve traffic routing and port conflicts

Ports are exclusive resources. If one container binds host port 8010, another container cannot use the same host port on the same machine. This creates two problems:

  • you cannot expose multiple applications on the same external port directly,
  • and you cannot easily scale components that publish host ports.

The standard solution is a reverse proxy. The reverse proxy is the single entry point that publishes external ports and routes traffic internally to the correct containers.

value.caption: Why a reverse proxy is needed
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming HTTP/HTTPS traffic] --> B[Reverse proxy]
    B --> C[App 1 containers]
    B --> D[App 2 containers]
    B --> E[API containers]
    B --> F[Other services]

body.value: Using NGINX as a reverse proxy

NGINX is a mature and extremely popular reverse proxy. It works well in containers, but it is traditionally configured with static files.

You define rules that map incoming hostnames or paths to backend services. NGINX receives the request, resolves the target service by DNS, fetches the content, and sends the response back to the client.

value.caption: Simple NGINX reverse proxy rule

body.value: One major advantage of NGINX is caching. If the response for a path is stable, NGINX can store it in memory or on disk and serve future requests without calling the application container again.

value.caption: Example NGINX cache usage

body.value:
This reduces latency and lowers application load, which is especially useful for expensive or mostly static responses.

The downside is operational: when routing rules change or service discovery changes, static configuration often means reloading or restarting the proxy.
Using Traefik as a dynamic reverse proxy

Traefik takes a more platform-aware approach. Instead of relying on static configuration files, it connects to the Docker or Kubernetes API and builds routing rules dynamically. In Docker, you typically expose containers to Traefik using labels.

value.caption: Traefik labels on a container
value.code:
services:
  whoami:
    image: my-whoami
    labels:
      - traefik.enable=true
      - traefik.http.routers.whoami.rule=Host(`whoami.local`)

body.value:
Traefik automatically detects containers, updates routing when replicas change, and can simplify SSL/TLS management considerably. It is especially strong at:

  • dynamic discovery,
  • automatic reconfiguration,
  • HTTPS termination,
  • middleware such as path rewriting and redirects.

Compared with NGINX, the trade-off is that Traefik does not traditionally offer the same caching feature set, though it handles API-driven environments very elegantly.

value.caption: Static vs dynamic reverse proxy model
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[NGINX] --> B[Static config files]
    B --> C[Manual reload or restart on changes]
    D[Traefik] --> E[Docker or Kubernetes API]
    E --> F[Dynamic routing updates]

body.value: HTTPS, redirects, and middleware

Reverse proxies are also the right place to centralize infrastructure concerns such as:

  • HTTP to HTTPS redirects,
  • TLS termination,
  • certificate management,
  • path rewriting,
  • sticky sessions,
  • rate limiting,
  • caching.

Traefik can integrate with certificate providers and even generate self-signed certificates for non-production use. NGINX can also terminate TLS, and in Kubernetes this is often combined with cert-manager.
Kubernetes Ingress: the platform-native reverse proxy model

Kubernetes treats reverse proxying as a first-class pattern. Instead of publishing every application directly, you usually:

  1. run applications behind internal ClusterIP services,
  2. run an Ingress controller such as NGINX or Traefik,
  3. define Ingress resources that map hostnames and paths to backend services.

This decouples application deployment from traffic routing.

value.caption: Traffic flow with Kubernetes Ingress
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Client] --> B[LoadBalancer Service]
    B --> C[Ingress Controller]
    C --> D[Ingress Rules]
    D --> E[ClusterIP Service]
    E --> F[Pods]

value.caption: Kubernetes Ingress example
value.code:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: whoami-ingress
spec:
  rules:
    - host: whoami.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: whoami-web
                port:
                  number: 80

body.value: With an NGINX Ingress controller, Kubernetes watches Ingress resources, discovers services and endpoints, and updates the NGINX configuration automatically. You get the power of NGINX without having to manually maintain static config files inside your own custom proxy container.
Ingress controllers can add useful production controls

An ingress controller is not just a request router. It is also a convenient place to apply production controls across applications, such as:

  • request rate limiting,
  • caching,
  • TLS termination,
  • header manipulation,
  • path-based routing,
  • host-based multi-tenant routing.

For example, rate limiting at the ingress layer can protect application services from abusive traffic before requests ever reach a pod.

value.caption: Ingress routing concept with separate website and API hosts
value.code:
www.apod.local   -> apod-web service
api.apod.local   -> apod-api service
whoami.local     -> whoami-web service

body.value: What a production-ready containerized application looks like

When these patterns are all in place, the result is not just a containerized app—it is an application that can participate properly in a production platform.

A production-ready containerized application typically has these properties:

  • the image contains the application and only environment-neutral defaults,
  • runtime configuration comes from environment variables, mounted files, ConfigMaps, or Secrets,
  • logs are visible as container logs and flow into centralized storage,
  • health and readiness are modeled explicitly,
  • incoming traffic is routed through a reverse proxy or ingress controller,
  • and all of this works without rebuilding images for each environment.

value.caption: End-to-end production architecture for containerized apps
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Immutable Docker Image] --> B[Platform injects config]
    B --> C[App runs in container or pod]
    C --> D[Logs to stdout]
    D --> E[Collector]
    E --> F[Searchable central log store]
    C --> G[Liveness and readiness probes]
    G --> H[Platform self-healing and rollout control]
    I[Ingress or reverse proxy] --> C
    I --> J[HTTPS, caching, routing, rate limiting]

body.value: Practical takeaways

value.text:
• Configuration: use layered config with defaults in the image, overrides from files, and final overrides from environment variables.
  • Legacy apps: use a Config Loader utility if the app only supports one config file.
  • Logging: write logs to stdout/stderr whenever possible.
  • Hidden sinks: use a relay or sidecar if the app writes logs to files or platform-specific sinks.
  • Health: add explicit health checks. Process alive is not enough.
  • Readiness: ensure pods do not receive traffic until they can actually do work.
  • Probe safety: do not make aggressive probes that overload third-party services.
  • Traffic: use a reverse proxy or ingress controller to front multiple apps and scale safely.
  • Promotion: move the same image through all environments and change behavior with platform configuration, not rebuilds.

body.value: Final summary

Containers become truly powerful in production when the platform can understand enough about what is happening inside them to manage them intelligently. That means the application must expose the right integration points.

Externalized configuration makes images portable. Console logging makes logs visible to the platform. Health and readiness checks make self-healing and safer rollouts possible. Reverse proxies and ingress controllers make multi-application hosting, HTTPS, and traffic routing manageable.

These patterns are not tied to one language. They work across Go, Node.js, Java, .NET, Linux containers, Windows containers, Docker Compose, Docker Swarm, and Kubernetes. Once you build them into your images and deployment model, your applications stop behaving like black boxes and start behaving like first-class citizens of the platform.

That is the real path from containerized to production-ready.

title: Troubleshooting Docker: Logs, Inspect, Filesystems, Build Cache, Volumes, Registries, and Networking
excerpt: A practical guide to troubleshooting Docker by working from first principles: read logs, inspect containers, verify files and permissions, understand build cache behavior, clean unused resources, decode exit codes, and systematically resolve ports, DNS, registry, certificate, volume, and network errors.

value.title: Docker Troubleshooting Starts with Observation, Not Guesswork

body.value:
When Docker workflows fail, the fastest path to a fix is rarely trial and error. The right approach is to narrow the problem systematically: inspect logs, inspect the container itself, verify file paths and permissions, confirm runtime configuration, and only then move deeper into builds, registries, mounts, or networking.

Most Docker issues fall into a few repeatable categories: runtime crashes, bad image builds, missing files, invalid mounts, registry authentication problems, and network misconfiguration. Once you know where to look first, troubleshooting becomes much more predictable.

value.title: Core Principle
value.text: Treat Docker errors as evidence. Logs, inspect output, exit codes, port bindings, mounted volumes, and network metadata usually tell you exactly where the failure lives.

value.caption: A practical troubleshooting flow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Error appears] --> B[Check container logs]
    B --> C{Enough information?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Fix app, command, config, or path]
    C -- No --> E[Inspect container metadata]
    E --> F[Verify image, command, mounts, ports, network]
    F --> G{Build problem?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Inspect Dockerfile and build cache]
    G -- No --> I{Connectivity problem?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Check ports, DNS, IPs, certificates, networks]
    I -- No --> K[Prune unused resources and retry cleanly]

value.title: Read the Logs Before You Change Anything

body.value:
Container logs should be your first stop for nearly every Docker issue. They show what the process inside the container wrote to standard output and standard error, which is often where application crashes, missing file errors, startup failures, and framework exceptions appear.

The most useful options are the ones that narrow output to what matters now: tailing recent lines, following live logs, showing timestamps, and filtering by time range.

value.caption: Common ways to read container logs
value.code:
docker logs my-container

docker logs --tail 100 my-container

docker logs -f my-container

docker logs --timestamps my-container
