docker logs --since 10m my-container

docker logs --until 2026-04-15T09:30:00Z my-container

body.value: If the logs immediately show a missing dependency, bad command, stack trace, or permissions error, you may already have the answer. If the logs are vague, move to inspection next rather than guessing.

value.title: Use docker inspect to See What the Container Is Actually Running

body.value:
The docker inspect command reveals runtime truth: the image in use, the startup command, restart count, log path, mounted volumes, network settings, port bindings, and host interaction details. This is one of the most important tools for understanding why a container behaves differently than expected.

Instead of staring at the full JSON every time, query only the fields you need with --format.

value.caption: Useful inspect queries
value.code:
docker inspect my-container

docker inspect --format '{{ .Id }}' my-container

docker inspect --format '{{ .Config.Image }}' my-container

docker inspect --format '{{ .LogPath }}' my-container

docker inspect --format '{{ json .Config }}' my-container

docker inspect --format '{{ json .NetworkSettings }}' my-container

docker inspect --format '{{ json .HostConfig }}' my-container

value.title: What to Check First in Inspect Output
value.text: Verify the image name, command, log path, mounted volumes, network mode, port bindings, restart count, and any runtime environment configuration. These fields expose most common misconfigurations quickly.

value.title: Verify Files, Paths, and Permissions Inside the Container

body.value:
Many Docker errors are really file system errors in disguise. A script may not exist at the path you think it does. A binary may be missing from the image. A file copied during build may have Windows line endings. A command may exist, but the current user cannot execute it.

The cleanest way to verify this is to open a shell inside the running container and inspect the file system directly.

value.caption: Open a shell and inspect the container file system
value.code:
docker exec -it my-container /bin/bash

# or, for smaller images
docker exec -it my-container /bin/sh

# once inside
pwd
ls -la
whoami
cat /etc/os-release

body.value: If the container is not running, you can still inspect its contents by exporting it to a TAR archive or copying files from it. This is useful when startup fails so early that an interactive shell is not possible.

value.caption: Inspect files from a non-running container
value.code:
docker export my-container -o my-container.tar

tar -tvf my-container.tar

docker cp my-container:/app ./container-app-copy

value.title: Common File Mistakes
value.text: Watch for wrong paths, using /bin/bash on images that only include /bin/sh, invalid line endings in shell scripts, and running scripts as a user that lacks execute permissions.

value.title: Keep Docker Clean or You Will Debug Ghosts

body.value:
Stopped containers, dangling images, unused networks, and abandoned volumes can create confusion. A failed experiment from yesterday can become a misleading conflict today. Cleaning resources regularly reduces the number of false leads when troubleshooting.

The prune family of commands removes unused Docker objects. Used carefully, they help restore a predictable environment.

value.caption: Useful prune commands
value.code:
docker image prune

docker image prune -a -f

docker container prune -f

docker network prune -f

docker volume prune -f

docker system prune -f

docker system prune --volumes -f

body.value: Images, containers, and networks can also be filtered in some prune commands by label or age. That makes cleanup safer in active environments, especially when not everything should be removed.

value.title: Understand Build Cache Before You Diagnose a Broken Image

body.value:
Docker caches image build layers to speed up rebuilds. That is efficient, but it also means you need to understand when Docker is reusing an earlier layer versus rebuilding one. A confusing build result is often explained by cache behavior, not by randomness.

For COPY and ADD, Docker uses a hash of files, directories, and permissions. If that hash changes, the step is rebuilt. Every following step is rebuilt as well because the previous layer changed.

value.caption: How cache invalidation flows through a Dockerfile
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[FROM python:3.12] --> B[WORKDIR /app]
    B --> C[COPY requirements.txt .]
    C --> D[RUN pip install -r requirements.txt]
    D --> E[COPY . .]
    E --> F[CMD python app.py]
    X[Change requirements.txt] --> C
    C --> Y[Cache miss]
    Y --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F

value.caption: Build commands that control cache behavior
value.code:
docker build -t my-image .

docker build --no-cache -t my-image .

docker build --pull -t my-image .

docker build --cache-from my-registry/app:latest -t my-image .

value.title: Troubleshooting Build Rule
value.text: When a build behaves unexpectedly, verify the order of Dockerfile instructions first. Dockerfile steps are executed in order, and one bad step often makes later failures look unrelated.

value.title: Use Intermediate Build Layers to Investigate Build Failures

body.value:
Each successful Dockerfile step produces an image layer. During debugging, that matters because you can often run a shell against the last successful intermediate image and inspect the environment just before the failure. In some cases, you can even create an image from a failed intermediate container.

This is especially useful when package installation, file copies, or build tools fail in the middle of a long Dockerfile.

value.caption: Example pattern for debugging build failures
value.code:
# Turn off BuildKit temporarily if you need more explicit intermediate output
# Docker Desktop Engine config example:
# {
#   "features": { "buildkit": false }
# }

# Build again and note the last successful image/container IDs

docker build -t my-image .

# Run a shell against a relevant intermediate image
docker run -it <intermediate-image-id> /bin/sh

# If needed, create an image from a failed intermediate container
docker commit <failed-container-id> debug-image
docker run -it debug-image /bin/sh

value.title: Registry Errors Usually Come Down to Names, Credentials, Proxies, or Rate Limits

body.value:
When image pulls fail, the error often looks more mysterious than it is. The most common causes are a misspelled image name, the wrong registry URL, missing login credentials, an unreachable registry behind a proxy, DNS resolution failure, or pull rate limits.

Private registries add another layer: credentials, trust, and secret management all need to be correct before pulls and pushes will work reliably.

value.caption: Registry troubleshooting commands
value.code:
docker login

docker trust inspect my-registry.example.com/team/app:latest

docker secret inspect my-secret

# Example daemon-related environment variables
# HTTP_PROXY
# HTTPS_PROXY
# NO_PROXY

value.title: Registry Checklist
value.text: Check image spelling, tag spelling, registry URL, authentication state, proxy settings, DNS behavior, and whether a public registry is enforcing pull rate limits for your account tier.

value.title: Exit Codes Tell You Where to Look Next

body.value:
Container exit codes are a compact but powerful hint. They can distinguish between a successful completion, an application crash, a missing command, a daemon-level problem, or a signal-based shutdown.

Instead of treating a stopped container as a mystery, inspect the exit code and pair that with the logs.

value.caption: Inspect a container exit code
value.code: docker inspect --format '{{ .State.ExitCode }}' my-container

value.caption: Common Docker exit codes
value.code:
0   = process completed successfully or no foreground process remains
1   = application error inside the container
125 = docker daemon or malformed docker command error
126 = command cannot be invoked
127 = command not found
137 = container received SIGKILL
139 = segmentation fault / memory access violation
143 = container received SIGTERM

value.title: Volume Problems Are Usually Path, Order, or Permission Problems

body.value:
Volume mount failures often produce the error invalid volume specification, but the root cause is usually simple: wrong source path, wrong target path, mounting a file where a directory is expected, reversed mount order, or user permissions that do not match the mounted directory.

Volumes deserve careful inspection because they affect both file visibility and data persistence.

value.caption: Volume inspection commands
value.code:
docker volume ls

docker volume inspect my-volume

docker inspect --format '{{ json .Config.Volumes }}' my-container

value.title: Mount Order Matters
value.text: If you mount a subfolder and later mount its parent over it, the later mount can obscure what was mounted first. Also be very cautious with root-level mounts when running with elevated permissions.

body.value:
Docker networking issues often look complicated because they blend host networking, container isolation, internal DNS, port publishing, and custom network configuration. The fastest way to regain clarity is to inspect both the network and the container’s view of that network.

The default bridge network works, but user-defined bridge networks are usually a better choice because they provide cleaner service discovery and more predictable behavior.

value.caption: Inspect Docker networks
value.code:
docker network ls

docker network inspect bridge

docker inspect --format '{{ json .NetworkSettings }}' my-container

docker inspect --format '{{ json .HostConfig }}' my-container

value.caption: How a published port connects host traffic to a container
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Browser or client on host] --> B[Host port 5000]
    B --> C[Docker port binding]
    C --> D[Container port 5000]
    D --> E[Application process inside container]

value.title: When a Port Is Already in Use, the Problem May Be Docker or the Host OS

body.value:
Errors about unavailable ports or failed external connectivity usually mean the same thing: the requested host port is already occupied. That conflict might come from another container or from a process running directly on the host operating system.

Start by checking running containers, then verify the host system itself.

value.caption: Check port mappings and host port usage
value.code:
docker inspect --format '{{ json .NetworkSettings.Ports }}' my-container

docker compose down

docker stop <container-id>

docker system prune -f

# macOS / Linux
sudo lsof -i -P -n | grep LISTEN

# Windows
netstat -nao

value.title: Test Name Resolution and Connectivity from Inside the Container

body.value:
Container-to-container communication depends on both network connectivity and name resolution. If a container can reach another one by IP but not by name, the likely problem is DNS. If it cannot reach either, the problem is usually the network or routing.

The best place to test this is from within the container itself.

value.caption: Test DNS and IP reachability from inside a container
value.code:
docker exec -it my-container /bin/sh

cat /etc/resolv.conf
ip addr show
ping other-container
ping 172.20.0.5
ping google.com

body.value: If needed, install missing networking tools inside a Debian or Ubuntu-based container during debugging.

value.caption: Install IP and ping tools in Ubuntu-based containers
value.code:
apt update
apt install -y iproute2 iputils-ping

value.title: DNS Tip
value.text: User-defined Docker networks provide built-in DNS for container names. The default bridge network is less convenient for this and is a common source of confusion in local development.

value.title: Certificate Errors Often Come from Trust Store Mismatch

body.value:
Certificate-related Docker failures often appear when pulling from registries or when TLS is used from inside containers. A common mistake is using the wrong certificate file extension or placing CA certificates in the wrong location. Another frequent issue is forgetting that Docker must be restarted after host certificate changes.

When Docker says a certificate was signed by an unknown authority, it usually means the CA certificate is not in the trust store Docker is actually using.

value.caption: Typical Linux container certificate update pattern
value.code:
COPY my-ca.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/my-ca.crt
RUN update-ca-certificates

body.value: On corporate networks, TLS inspection may also require the organization’s CA certificate rather than a locally created one.

value.title: Network Not Found Usually Means Containers Point to a Removed Network ID

body.value:
Docker Compose creates networks automatically. If containers are stopped but not removed, the network can be deleted while those containers still reference it. Later, when you restart the containers, the network name may exist again but the network ID will be different. That is how you end up with a network not found error even though the network appears to exist.

The fix is to remove both the stale containers and the stale network state, then recreate everything cleanly.

value.caption: Why a recreated network can still break existing containers
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Compose creates network N1] --> B[Containers attach to N1]
    B --> C[Containers are stopped but not removed]
    C --> D[Network N1 is removed]
    D --> E[Compose creates new network with same name but ID N2]
    E --> F[Old containers still reference N1]
    F --> G[network not found error]
    G --> H[Remove stale containers and recreate]

value.caption: Clean recovery for network-not-found scenarios
value.code:
docker compose down

docker system prune -f

# optional but often useful
# restart Docker Desktop

docker compose up --force-recreate

value.title: Healthy Habit
value.text: At the end of a work session, bringing projects down cleanly and pruning unused Docker resources reduces the chance of stale networks, stopped containers, and confusing port conflicts the next time you start.

value.title: A Repeatable Checklist for Troubleshooting Docker

body.value:
For most Docker problems, this sequence works well:

  1. Read the container logs.
  2. Inspect the container image, command, mounts, ports, and network settings.
  3. Open a shell and verify files, paths, line endings, and permissions.
  4. If it is a build issue, inspect Dockerfile order and cache behavior.
  5. If it is a registry issue, verify names, tags, login, trust, DNS, and proxy settings.
  6. If it is a volume issue, inspect mount points and user permissions.
  7. If it is a networking issue, inspect networks, test ports, test DNS, and test IP reachability from inside the container.
  8. When in doubt, clean unused resources and retry in a fresh state.

value.title: Docker Becomes Easier Once You Know Where the Truth Lives

body.value:
Docker troubleshooting is not about memorizing every error string. It is about knowing where runtime truth is stored and how to interrogate it. Logs tell you what failed. Inspect output tells you how the container was configured. Shell access tells you what files and tools actually exist. Build cache explains why a change did or did not take effect. Networks, ports, DNS, and certificates explain how containers communicate.

Once you work this way consistently, most Docker problems stop feeling mysterious. They become a series of concrete checks that lead you to the fix.

value.title: Final Takeaway
value.text: The most effective Docker troubleshooting habit is to move from evidence to hypothesis, not from frustration to guesswork.
